We have
P: "I am rich"
Q: "I got famous through a game which attract many young individuals".
R: "I have the urge"
So hear me out. We know that if R happens then it implies either P or Q is true.
That's R --> (P v Q).
Based on Discrete Mathematics's theory about Implications in Logical propositions.
It states that if:
- R --> (P v Q) is True
- R is true
=> (P v Q) have to be true.
So the conclusion is, if you were to develop the urge, it is a guarantee, that you will either become super rich, become famous through a game usually played by young individuals, or both.
The recipe for success was in our hands all this time!
I'm P yet I f#cking despise those little devils.

But if you're P, as in P is true. Then (P v Q) is true.

Nvm, it also state that even if R is true, (P v Q) don't necessarily have to be because the proposition (P v Q) --> R is only wrong if  (P v Q) is true while R is wrong.
However, that means if you're rich or famous, it is guaranteed that your R is true
